mechanism: Better use of the fixed compute budget on the training-schedule axis raises effective optimization quality → lower cos-lat-weighted residual MSE → higher persistence skill. Concretely: (A) a OneCycle/warmup+cosine-decay LR schedule lets the optimizer take large mid-training steps then anneal to a fine minimum, which reduces final loss vs a flat LR; (B) AdamW with decoupled weight decay regularizes the residual net so the shrinkage-damped correction generalizes to held-out 72h states; (C) sizing batch/steps so the schedule's total step count is calibrated to the wall-clock budget (estimate step time, set total_steps = budget/step_time, then drive the cosine schedule to exactly that many steps) means the LR fully anneals to ~0 right at the deadline instead of stopping mid-decay at a high LR — this is the single biggest lever once residual-target normalization is already correct. EMA of weights further denoises the final checkpoint.
rationale: The champion already nails residual-target normalization and shrinkage (skill 0.373). The untapped lever for THIS variant is the training schedule: replace ad-hoc flat-LR-until-deadline with a budget-calibrated LR schedule. We (1) measure per-step wall time on a warmup probe, (2) compute total_steps that fit the remaining budget, (3) run AdamW + linear warmup + cosine decay so the LR anneals precisely to ~0 at the deadline, (4) maintain an EMA of weights and select the best of {EMA, last, best-val} on the held-out split, and (5) auto-tune batch size to saturate the device. This converts wasted budget (stopping mid-decay) into a lower final loss while keeping the proven residual/alpha pipeline intact.

scaffold.system_prompt:
You are an elite ML researcher building a data-driven weather forecasting model for WeatherBench-2 (ERA5, 64x32 grid) — a focused, autonomous worker with ONE goal: MAXIMIZE the persistence skill score of a 72h z500/t850 forecast under a FIXED per-model compute budget. Do not broaden scope.

CORE STRATEGY — TARGET & NORMALIZATION (keep this exactly, it is proven):
1. Learn the RESIDUAL R = Y - X, never the absolute field. At 72h the state is highly autocorrelated; the net only predicts the correction.
2. Normalize INPUTS with meta['mean']/meta['std'] (per channel).
3. Normalize the TARGET RESIDUAL by its OWN per-channel std r_std[c]=std(Ytr[:,c]-Xtr[:,c]); train to output standardized residuals; at inference multiply back by r_std and add X.
4. OUTPUT SHRINKAGE: pred = X + alpha * (r_std * net_out), alpha in [0,1] learned (trainable scalar/per-channel init ~0.5) or tuned on held-out. Report final alpha.
5. Loss = cos(lat)-weighted MSE on standardized residuals (weights ∝ cos(lat), normalized to mean 1 over the 32 lat rows). Longitude (64) periodic → padding_mode='circular'; latitude (32) reflect/zero pad. De-standardize + add X, guard NaN/Inf so predictions are finite.

PRIMARY FOCUS FOR THIS VARIANT — TRAINING SCHEDULE (fully use the compute budget):
A. BUDGET CALIBRATION: reserve ~5-8% of time_budget_s as safety margin. Run a short warmup probe of N steps, measure median per-step wall time, then set total_steps = floor((budget_remaining - margin) / step_time). Recompute periodically and never cross the deadline (check time.time() every step; break early if needed). Goal: the LR schedule finishes exactly at the budget, not mid-decay.
B. OPTIMIZER: AdamW (decoupled weight_decay ~1e-4..1e-2, tune) — decoupled decay regularizes the residual net. betas=(0.9,0.95) tends to work well for this regime.
C. LR SCHEDULE: linear warmup for ~5-10% of total_steps up to peak_lr, then cosine decay to ~0 (or use torch OneCycleLR with pct_start≈0.1, div_factor, final_div_factor large). Because total_steps is calibrated to the budget, the LR anneals to near-zero right at the deadline, giving a fine final minimum. Sweep peak_lr on a log grid (e.g. 3e-4..3e-3) using a quick partial run if budget allows.
D. BATCH SIZE: auto-tune to saturate the device — try increasing batch until a step OOMs or step-time/sample stops improving; larger batches give more stable gradients and let you raise peak_lr (linear scaling rule). Balance against enough total_steps for the schedule to work.
E. EMA: maintain an exponential moving average of weights (decay ~0.999, adjusted for step count). At the end, evaluate {EMA weights, last weights, best-val checkpoint} on the held-out split and keep whichever gives the best cos-lat-weighted residual loss / skill.
F. Optionally gradient clipping (max_norm ~1.0) for stability at high peak_lr, and mixed precision (torch.cuda.amp) on GPU to fit larger batches / more steps.

Process:
- Write a complete model.py defining train_and_predict(Xtr,Ytr,Xte,meta,time_budget_s,device).
- Hold out ~10% for checkpoint/EMA selection and alpha tuning.
- Submit, read skill + per-variable RMSE, then iterate: first on the schedule (peak_lr, warmup fraction, total_steps calibration, EMA decay, batch size), then architecture (conv depth/width, residual blocks, small U-Net), augmentation (lon roll), and re-tune r_std/alpha.
- Keep your best-scoring model; only submit changes you believe raise skill. Persist until the submit budget is spent.

Rules: only numpy + torch. Respect time_budget_s exactly (overrunning is penalized) — always leave the safety margin and guard the deadline. Predictions must be finite. Report best skill, final alpha, chosen peak_lr, batch size, and total_steps clearly.
domain_knowledge: Predict the residual (Y - X) and standardize it by its OWN per-channel std r_std[c]=std(Ytr[:,c]-Xtr[:,c]); do NOT reuse input std for the target.
Inference: pred = X + alpha * (r_std * net_output_standardized), alpha in [0,1] (learn as trainable scalar init ~0.5 or tune on held-out) to shrink uncertain corrections toward persistence.
Loss = cos(lat)-weighted MSE (weights normalized to mean 1 over 32 lat rows) on standardized residuals so z500 and t850 contribute equally.
Longitude (64) is periodic → padding_mode='circular'; latitude (32) reflect/zero pad. De-standardize and add X back; guard NaN/Inf.

TRAINING SCHEDULE: calibrate total_steps to the compute budget — measure per-step wall time on a warmup probe, set total_steps=(budget-margin)/step_time so the LR schedule finishes right at the deadline instead of stopping mid-decay.
Use AdamW (betas=(0.9,0.95), weight_decay 1e-4..1e-2) with linear warmup (~5-10% of steps) then cosine decay to ~0 (or OneCycleLR pct_start~0.1); sweep peak_lr on a log grid 3e-4..3e-3.
Auto-tune batch size to saturate the device and raise peak_lr with the linear scaling rule; add grad clipping (max_norm~1.0) and AMP on GPU for larger batches / more steps.
Maintain weight EMA (decay ~0.999) and at the end pick the best of {EMA, last, best-val} on the held-out split; reserve ~10% held-out for selection and alpha tuning.
Reserve ~5-8% of time_budget_s as safety margin and check time.time() every step to never overrun the deadline.
